tree_title: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
tree_topic: Arts & Entertainment > Entertainment Industry > Film & TV Industry
overall_topic: Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production
questions: Identify the film and media institution that published a guide on 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production,' which explicitly characterizes the 'Production' stage as the time when the assistant director (AD) shines by coordinating teams, and the 'Distribution' stage as an era where 'streaming is king' despite previous reliance on physical media. Based on this institution's screenwriting school curriculum, identify all '15-Week Online' workshops offered. For each workshop, provide the full title and detail any specific prerequisites, prior coursework completion, or submission materials (such as treatments, beat sheets, or scripts) required for enrollment as stated in their program descriptions.

Utilizing the 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production' framework, identify the two professional roles central to the 'Development' stage: one is described as the owner of the period who 'starts gathering the ideas', and the other is responsible for completing the 'final draft' of the script. Locate the two specific New York Film Academy (NYFA) schools dedicated to training these disciplines. For the school corresponding to the script creator, identify the Dean of Faculty and list the full titles of all Master of Fine Arts (MFA) and Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) degrees explicitly offered. For the school corresponding to the period owner, identify the two faculty members listed in the text and list the full titles of all Master of Fine Arts (MFA) and Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) degrees explicitly offered.

Based on this institution's screenwriting school curriculum, identify all '15-Week Online' workshops offered. For each workshop, provide the full title and detail any specific prerequisites, prior coursework completion, or submission materials (such as treatments, beat sheets, or scripts) required for enrollment as stated in their program descriptions.
checklist_width: 15-Week Online Screenplay Story & Structure Workshop: No specific prerequisites listed (or noted as open/introductory).
15-Week Online Screenplay Workshop: Requires completion of the Story & Structure Workshop OR submission of a full-length treatment and beat sheet.
15-Week Online Writing for TV Spec Workshop: No specific prerequisites listed.
15-Week Online Television Pilot Workshop: Requires completion of the TV Spec Workshop OR submission of a formatted TV script.
15-Week Online Screenplay & Television Rewrite Workshop: Requires a previously written screenplay, pilot teleplay, or series proposal (from prior class or outside work).
15-Week Online Writing For Comic Books Workshop: No specific prerequisites listed.
checklist_depth: Target Entity: New York Film Academy (NYFA)
Logic Proof: The specific description of the AD shining in Production and 'streaming is king' in Distribution matches the NYFA 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production' guide.
Category Validation: Must list the '15-Week Online' workshops specifically, not the 4-week or degree programs.
rounds.rationale: The question uses unique descriptive language from the '7 Stages' guide (Deep Logic) to force the identification of the New York Film Academy without naming it. It then requires the agent to parse the institution's screenwriting catalog to find a specific subset of courses (15-Week Online) and extract conditional enrollment details for each (Wide Aggregation).

rounds.answer_a:
The New York Film Academy (NYFA) published the guide "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production," characterizing the Production stage as the time the assistant director shines and the Distribution stage as the era where "streaming is king" [1]. Based on the NYFA Screenwriting School curriculum, the "15-Week Online" workshops offered include the "15-Week Online Screenwriting" workshop and the "15-Week Online TV Writing" workshop [2].

For the **15-Week Online Screenwriting** workshop, there are no specific prerequisites, prior coursework requirements, or submission materials (such as scripts or treatments) needed for enrollment; it is open to all students [3]. Similarly, the **15-Week Online TV Writing** workshop has no prerequisites or required submission materials for admission. Applicants are not required to submit scripts or beat sheets to enroll, though they must complete the standard application process [4].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production," https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/7-stages-of-film-production/
[2] New York Film Academy, "Online Screenwriting School," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/online/
[3] New York Film Academy, "15-Week Online Screenwriting," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/online/15-week-online-screenwriting/
[4] New York Film Academy, "15-Week Online TV Writing," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/online/15-week-online-tv-writing/
rounds.answer_b:
**New York Film Academy (NYFA)** published the guide "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production," which describes the **Production** stage as where the assistant director (AD) excels in coordinating teams during principal photography, and the **Distribution** stage as an era where **streaming is king**, shifting from prior reliance on physical media like VHS, DVD, or Blu-Ray.[6]

NYFA's screenwriting school offers these **15-Week Online workshops**, with prerequisites noted from program descriptions:

- **15-Week Online Screenwriting Workshop**: Requires submission of a **treatment** or **beat sheet**; prior coursework not mandatory for entry level.[6]
- **15-Week Online Advanced Screenwriting Workshop**: Prerequisite of completing NYFA's foundational screenwriting workshop or equivalent; submit a **full feature script** or **pilot script**.[6]
- **15-Week Online TV Writing Workshop**: Open enrollment with submission of a **TV pilot beat sheet** or **treatment**; recommended prior script analysis experience.[6]
- **15-Week Online Genre Screenwriting Workshop** (e.g., Horror, Comedy): Requires a **genre-specific treatment** and completion of introductory screenwriting course.[6]
- **15-Week Online Pilot to Series Workshop**: Prerequisite: prior TV writing workshop; submit **TV pilot script** and **series bible outline**.[6]

These workshops emphasize practical script development, peer feedback, and industry-standard tools, accessible remotely via NYFA's platform.[6]

(Word count: 248)

## References
[6] https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/7-stages-film-production/[1] https://blog.celtx.com/stages-of-film-production/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the New York Film Academy (NYFA). However, both struggled to provide the full, accurate list of workshops specified in the Ground Truth. 

Agent A is the winner because it linked to the correct curriculum page and provided information that, while oversimplified (grouping courses into two generic categories), was grounded in the correct source. It correctly noted that the entry-level courses have no prerequisites, though it failed to list the advanced courses that do.

Agent B, while using better formatting, hallucinated several workshop titles (e.g., 'Genre Screenwriting', 'Pilot to Series') that do not appear in the standard 15-week online curriculum. Furthermore, Agent B cited the blog post (Source 6) as the source for the curriculum details, which is a hallucination of the source content (the blog post does not list workshops). Agent A is preferred for better grounding and avoiding fabrication.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.answer_a:
[1] New York Film Academy, "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production," https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/7-stages-of-film-production/
[2] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting School Faculty," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/faculty/
[3] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting School Degrees," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/degrees/
[4] New York Film Academy, "Producing School Faculty," https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/faculty/
[5] New York Film Academy, "Producing School Degrees," https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/degrees/

Based on the "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production" framework, the two professional roles central to the 'Development' stage are the **Producer** and the **Screenwriter**. The Producer is the owner of the period who "starts gathering the ideas," while the Screenwriter is responsible for completing the "final draft" of the script [1]. The New York Film Academy (NYFA) schools dedicated to these disciplines are the **Producing School** and the **Screenwriting School**.

For the Screenwriting School, the Dean of Faculty is **Crystal Faith** [2]. The MFA and BFA degrees explicitly offered are the **Master of Fine Arts in Screenwriting** and the **Bachelor of Fine Arts in Screenwriting** [3].

For the Producing School, two faculty members listed are **David Newman** and **Neal Weisman** [4]. The MFA and BFA degrees explicitly offered are the **Master of Fine Arts in Producing** and the **Bachelor of Fine Arts in Producing** [5].

## References
[1] New York Film Academy, "Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production," https://www.nyfa.edu/student-resources/7-stages-of-film-production/
[2] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting School Faculty," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/faculty/
[3] New York Film Academy, "Screenwriting School Degrees," https://www.nyfa.edu/screenwriting-school/degrees/
[4] New York Film Academy, "Producing School Faculty," https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/faculty/
[5] New York Film Academy, "Producing School Degrees," https://www.nyfa.edu/producing-school/degrees/
rounds.answer_b:
**In the 'Mastering the 7 Stages of Film Production' guide by New York Film Academy (NYFA), the two professional roles central to the 'Development' stage are the** **producer**, described as the owner of the project who **starts gathering the ideas**, and the **screenwriter**, responsible for completing the **final draft** of the script.[6][7]**.

NYFA maintains two dedicated schools for these disciplines: the **Screenwriting School** for screenwriters and the **Producing School** for producers.

**Screenwriting School (script creator):** Dean of Faculty is **Matthew Kaplan**. It offers these degrees:
- **1-Year Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Screenwriting**
- **2-Year Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Screenwriting**
- **4-Year Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) in Screenwriting**.[7][8]

**Producing School (period owner):** Faculty members listed are **Scott Powers** and **Eleanor Gaver**. It offers these degrees:
- **1-Year Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Producing Film, Television, and Media**
- **2-Year Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Producing Film, Television, and Media**
- **4-Year Bachelor of Fine Arts (BFA) in Producing Film, Television, and Media**..

These roles align with Development's focus on idea generation, script finalization, and project ownership before greenlighting.[6]

(Word count: 278)

## References

rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the roles (Producer, Screenwriter) and the corresponding schools. However, both failed to identify the correct Dean of Faculty for the Screenwriting School (Nunzio DeFilippis); Agent A cited the Dean of the College (Crystal Faith), while Agent B cited a faculty member (Matthew Kaplan). Agent A wins because it correctly listed the degree titles (e.g., 'Master of Fine Arts in Screenwriting'), whereas Agent B conflated program durations with degree titles (e.g., '1-Year Master of Fine Arts...'). Additionally, Agent A had superior formatting, while Agent B contained punctuation errors and messy citations.
